router-5._note: No hosts/switch attached in this scenario (router_id 5 has no entries in switches_detail). ip4/lan_subnet left null rather than the XML's unexplained 10.0.0.5/16 -- that value doesn't fit the /24-gateway convention every other router uses and isn't backed by a switches_detail entry, so it looks like planner leftover rather than a real LAN. Confirm before assigning any LAN IP here.

topology._traffic_note: The XML's Traffic <section> (pattern=burst, period_s=2.0, jitter_pct=35.0) is the density-based generation intent; traffic_summary.flows (pattern=continuous, period_s=10.0, jitter_pct=0.0) is what the planner actually resolved into a concrete flow. Used the resolved flow values here, matching how Scenario3's topology.traffic was populated from resolved flows rather than the section's macro parameters. Flag this to the designer if burst/128kbps/2s was the intended in-game behavior -- as written only one flow (pc-3 -> docker-1) is instantiated; the 'predicted_allow_rules' entries (docker-2->server-4, pc-3->server-4, server-4->docker-1) are preview-only synthetic allow-rule suggestions, not actual traffic flows, and were not added to this list.

flags._note: Superseded by Challenge200_solution.json / Challenge200_vulns.json, uploaded after the initial pass. The XML's own FlagSequencing said flags_enabled=false with an empty flag_assignments list, but the solution file gives concrete resolved flag values and an edge docker-1(6) -> docker-2(7), so that's now treated as authoritative and topology-flags.yml is wired back in.
